query: differential diagnosis of medial concavity in neonates with improved alignment on stimulation

retrieved_docs.title: Craniofacial Distraction Osteogenesis -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Mandibular Distraction: The most common indication for mandibular distraction is lengthening of the mandibular body in neonates and children with airway compromise due to severe micrognathia or retrognathia. For this indication, the osteotomy should be placed at the junction of the mandibular body and angle to avoid injury to tooth buds. The lateral, superior, and inferior cortices of the mandible are cut (both traditional saws and piezo-electric/ultrasonic cutters can be used), and a bone spreader is used to fracture and release the posterior cortex. This is to avoid injury to the inferior alveolar neurovascular bundle in its canal. The osteotomy can be made via and intra-oral, extra-oral, or combined approach. The desired device is then affixed to the bone segments proximally and distally and tested to ensure free mobility across the osteotomy.

retrieved_docs.title: Foot Drop in Obstetrics -- Differential Diagnosis -- Lesions of the Lumbosacral Trunk (Part of the Lumbosacral Plexus)
retrieved_docs.content: Electromyography helps make the diagnosis of a lesion of the lumbosacral trunk. Voluntary motor unit action potentials (MUAPs) of obstetric patients exhibiting foot droop have been shown to be significantly reduced in all the muscles below the knee that contain the L5 segment:  tibialis anterior, extensor hallucis, extensor digitorum brevis, fibularis longus, tibialis posterior, and flexor digitorum longus.

retrieved_docs.title: Gestational Age Assessment -- Technique or Treatment -- Postnatal Techniques
retrieved_docs.content: New Ballard Score: This improved scoring system is used to determine postnatal gestational age in infants as preterm as 20 weeks. The system is divided into 6 physical maturity components and six neuromuscular components. The physical maturity components include skin, lanugo, plantar creases, breast, ear/eye, and genitals. The neuromuscular components include posture, square window/wrist, arm recoil, popliteal angle, scarf sign, and heel to ear. Detailed illustrations have been created to assist the examiner in determining appropriate scores which correlate with gestational age. This exam is quicker to perform and may be more tolerable for sicker infants. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Foot Alignment Deformities -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Congenital vertical talus: CVT should be differentiated from congenital oblique talus. The lateral radiograph will depict no parallel longitudinal axes of the talus and first metatarsal in both conditions. In congenital oblique talus, however, with forced plantarflexion, the talonavicular joint is reduced, so the parallel longitudinal axes of the talus and first metatarsal become parallel, meaning that it is a flexible deformity. Other differentials include tarsal coalition and pes planovalgus. [32] [63] Congenital oblique talus: Differential diagnoses include congenital vertical talus (forced plantarflexion does not cause reduction of the talonavicular joint) and pes planovalgus. [35] Metatarsus adductus: Due to the distinct in-toeing gait, the metatarsus adductus can be confused with internal tibial torsion and increased femoral anteversion. [105] Skew foot: Skewfoot may be differentiated from simple or complex metatarsus adductus, equinovarus, and pes planus. [40] [79]

retrieved_docs.title: Esotropia -- Treatment / Management -- Infantile/Congenital Esotropia
retrieved_docs.content: The ideal time for surgical intervention in these patients is between 6 months to 2 years of age. The final decision should be taken based on the child's co-operation to measure squint and understanding of the parents and the examiner. Choice of surgery also varies depending upon the surgeons' preference. Bimedial recessions are usually preferred over unilateral recession/resect procedures. If significant inferior oblique overactions are associated, combining bimedial recession/ unilateral recession-resection with bilateral inferior oblique muscles weakening is preferable. The surgery for dissociated vertical deviation should be deferred as a primary procedure and can be combined later as a staged procedure if needed.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Foot Alignment Deformities -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Skew foot: Anteroposterior (AP) and lateral foot radiographs need to confirm the diagnosis (Media item 7). In the AP radiograph, the talocalcaneal angle will be increased (normal 20 to 40 degrees). The 1 metatarsal angle described by Barriolhet is increased (normal 50 to 70 degrees) and reveals forefoot adduction. The medial cuneiform medially deviates, while the navicular may be laterally deviated in the AP radiograph. In the lateral foot radiograph, the navicular bone may be dorsiflexed and medial cuneiform plantarflexed. [40] [41] [79] MRI or CT may also be useful in preoperative planning. Their disadvantage, however, is that they can not be performed with the patient standing, so weight-bearing angles cannot be measured. [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Foot Drop in Obstetrics -- Differential Diagnosis -- Identifying a Common Fibular Neuropathy
retrieved_docs.content: If the patient presentation aligns with these characteristics, conducting an EMG can aid in confirming a diagnosis of common fibular neuropathy. Electromyographic changes should be limited to the tibialis anterior, extensor digitorum longus, and extensor hallucis longus for the deep fibular nerve, the fibularis longus, and brevis for the superficial fibular nerve, and both for the common fibular nerve.

retrieved_docs.title: Strabismus -- Differential Diagnosis -- Congenital Esotropia
retrieved_docs.content: Early-onset accommodative esotropia Abducens palsy Nystagmus blockage syndrome Duane retraction syndrome Sensory esotropia Strabismus fixus Moebius syndrome [86]

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Foot Alignment Deformities -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the most common pediatric foot alignment deformities. Determine the etiology of the most common pediatric foot deformities. Implement the proper diagnostic approach including a thorough medical, family, antenatal, and birth history for each type of deformity. Apply best practices in the treatment of pediatric foot deformities. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Blount Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Acute Correction
retrieved_docs.content: Children aged 3 or older, regardless of the stage, or patients with stage III Blount disease, irrespective of age, are considered suitable for an osteotomy. Accurate measurement of limb alignment after acute or gradual correction can be challenging. To visualize mechanical axis alignment, some researchers utilize intraoperative fluoroscopy with the electrocautery cord placed across the skin, overlaying the center of the hip and ankle. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Foot Alignment Deformities -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Congenital oblique talus: Anteroposterior (AP) and lateral radiograph of the foot are required (Media item 6). In the AP radiograph, the talocalcaneal angle will be increased (normal 20-40 degrees). In the lateral radiograph, the talus's longitudinal axis passes below the first metatarsal axis with dorsal subluxation of the talonavicular joint. With forced lateral flexion, however, the talonavicular joint is reduced, and the axes of the talus and first metatarsal become parallel because of its flexible deformity. Further evaluation (MRI, nerve conduction studies, genetic testing) may be required to exclude neuromuscular or genetic causes of COT. [35] [73]

retrieved_docs.title: Plagiocephaly -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Facts noted in history are the presence at birth, preferred sleep position, and improvement with time. [23] Craniosynostosis is congenital, whereas deformational plagiocephaly usually develops in the neonatal period. History of a preferred sleep position will be present in cases of deformational plagiocephaly. The deformity in synostosis worsens with time. The deformities caused by position improve as the child develops head control.

retrieved_docs.title: Esotropia -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Congenital sixth nerve palsy – These can present as unilateral or bilateral cases. Doll's head maneuver reveals limited abduction based on laterality.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Foot Alignment Deformities -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Pediatric foot deformities may share similar characteristics, and their clinical pictures may resemble other foot entities. Therefore, it is essential to distinguish among different pediatric foot deformities.

retrieved_docs.title: Akinesia -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Osteogenesis imperfecta: This is typically associated with multiple fractures, not accompanied by contractures, unlike fetal akinesia syndrome. Other findings, like blue sclera, conductive deafness, and mild stunting, would make this diagnosis more likely. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Foot Alignment Deformities -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Clubfoot: The diagnosis of clubfoot is clinical; therefore, a radiograph examination is not always performed. Moreover, on some occasions, clubfoot may be diagnosed antenatally. If x-rays are performed, the dorsiflexion lateral (Turco) and anteroposterior (AP) views are ordered (Media item 2). In the Turco view, there is more hindfoot (between the calcaneus and the talus) parallelism, with a talocalcaneal angle of less than 25. The AP radiograph also reveals hindfoot parallelism, with a talocalcaneal angle (Kite angle) of less than 20 (normal angle is 20-40 degrees). [76] Ultrasound is also helpful when diagnosing clubfoot antenatally. [76]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2920
retrieved_docs.content: Intrapartum Fetal Stimulation and Manual Fetal Rotation are both classified as إجراءات توليدية (obstetric procedures).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1993
retrieved_docs.content: Cesarean hysterectomy, Intrapartum Fetal Stimulation, Manual Fetal Rotation, and بزل السائل الأمنيوسي (amniocentesis) are all classified as إجراءات توليدية (obstetric procedures). Cesarean hysterectomy involves removal of the uterus during a cesarean delivery, Intrapartum Fetal Stimulation is used to assess fetal well‑being during labor, and Manual Fetal Rotation assists in correcting an abnormal fetal position to facilitate vaginal delivery. Amniocentesis, identified as a child procedure of the broader إجراءات توليدية category, entails sampling amniotic fluid for diagnostic evaluation of fetal health. Collectively, these procedures represent the spectrum of interventions employed in maternal‑fetal care to manage delivery, diagnose fetal conditions, and address obstetric complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2373
retrieved_docs.content: Cesarean hysterectomy, Intrapartum Fetal Stimulation, Manual Fetal Rotation, and بضع الرحم are all classified as إجراء توليدي (obstetric procedures). Within the broader category of إجراء توليدي, مراقبة الجنين (fetal monitoring) is identified as a child procedure, indicating that fetal monitoring functions as a component or sub‑procedure of obstetric care. Recognizing that these specific interventions belong to the same procedural family and that they commonly incorporate fetal monitoring helps clinicians coordinate care, ensure fetal well‑being, and apply appropriate obstetric management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2327
retrieved_docs.content: Application of therapeutic agent and delivery of vertex presentation are both classified as child procedures within the broader category of surgical manipulation, indicating that these specific interventions are considered subtypes of surgical manipulation in medical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2345
retrieved_docs.content: Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus is classified as a subtype within the broader category described as Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable, indicating that this fetal presentation falls under a parent classification of transverse or oblique presentations without a defined episode of care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2314
retrieved_docs.content: Malposition; malpresentation is classified as a protracted (prolapsed) arm of the fetus, with the episode of care unspecified or not applicable.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2904
retrieved_docs.content: Malposition; malpresentation are specific manifestations of fetal position instability (عدم استقرار وضعية الجنين). Both terms describe abnormal fetal positioning that can compromise the progress of labor and increase the risk of delivery complications. Recognizing that malposition and malpresentation are subcategories of fetal position instability helps clinicians anticipate potential problems, monitor the pregnancy more closely, and plan appropriate obstetric interventions to ensure safer outcomes for both mother and infant.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 997
retrieved_docs.content: positioning methods, and a spectrum of complications—including cervical dystocia, uterine atony, prolonged or stimulated labor, and pelvic‑malformation‑related difficult deliveries—providing a comprehensive framework for understanding labor‑related care and its associated indications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2338
retrieved_docs.content: Malposition; malpresentation is classified as a transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable. The specific condition Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus has the same transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable as its parent classification. Both entities therefore fall under the broader category of fetal malposition in which the fetus lies horizontally or at an oblique angle, a classification that does not differentiate between particular episodes of care. Recognizing that malposition; malpresentation and Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus belong to this generic transverse or oblique presentation category is important for obstetric assessment and guides appropriate management strategies such as attempts at version or planning for operative delivery.

Collectively, these entities and relationships emphasize the importance of comprehensive prenatal assessment, careful monitoring of fetal presentation and pelvic dimensions, avoidance of contraindicated medications, and individualized delivery planning to optimize maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1226

This integrated representation supports clinicians in identifying, categorizing, and managing various abnormal fetal presentations to optimize maternal‑fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2904
retrieved_docs.content: Internal obstetrical version and manual conversion of position are both specific procedures classified as surgical manipulation techniques. Each of these methods is considered a child procedure within the broader category of surgical manipulation, reflecting their role as specialized interventions used to alter fetal position during obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 172
retrieved_docs.content: reconstructions—are organized under “other procedures associated with delivery.” Overall, the framework interrelates antenatal/postnatal care, multilingual terminology, instrumental deliveries (forceps, vacuum, breech extraction), fetal‑head manipulations (manual rotation, conversion, versions), induction methods (AROM, membrane sweeping, therapeutic agents), and tissue‑repair procedures, providing a structured model for comprehensive maternal‑fetal management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2132
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical Manipulation is a broad category of operative techniques that encompasses several specific procedures. Among these, the Application of therapeutic agent refers to the intra‑operative delivery of medication or biologic substances to treat disease directly at the surgical site. The Delivery of vertex presentation describes an obstetric maneuver used during childbirth to assist the passage of a fetus presenting head‑first, often requiring careful manual handling to ensure safe delivery. Manual conversion of position denotes a technique in which a clinician manually repositions a patient’s body part or the entire patient to achieve optimal anatomical alignment for subsequent treatment or to correct malposition. Together, these three child procedures illustrate the diverse ways in which surgical manipulation can be employed to administer therapy, facilitate delivery, and adjust anatomical positioning, all of which are integral to effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2732
retrieved_docs.content: Application of therapeutic agent, Combined obstetrical version, and Manual conversion of position are all specific types of surgical manipulation. As child procedures of the broader category of surgical manipulation, each involves an operative technique aimed at altering a physiological condition during a medical intervention. Application of therapeutic agent refers to the intra‑operative delivery of medication to achieve a therapeutic effect directly at the target site. Combined obstetrical version denotes a coordinated obstetric maneuver performed surgically to reposition a fetus to a more favorable presentation for delivery. Manual conversion of position describes a hands‑on technique used during surgery to adjust the patient’s or organ’s orientation to improve access or outcomes. Recognizing these procedures as subcategories of surgical manipulation helps clinicians select appropriate operative strategies, anticipate procedural requirements, and optimize patient care through targeted therapeutic and positional interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2444
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal distress and abnormal forces of labor are identified as a specific form of ضائقة جنينية تؤثر في علاج الأم, indicating that this type of fetal compromise not only reflects problems with the fetus but also has direct implications for the management and therapeutic decisions concerning the mother. Recognizing that fetal distress and abnormal mechanical forces during labor can affect maternal treatment underscores the importance of coordinated obstetric care that addresses both fetal well‑being and maternal health outcomes.
